result:
A quietly persistent midnight moth beats against the lamp in my office.  The faint ticking of the vintage wall clock is more heartbeat than mechanism, keeping time not for the world but for this single room.  Somewhere beyond my window a freight train rolls through the valley, its cars clanking like sleepy voyageurs; the doppler shift of its whistle makes the entire house feel like a boat drifting sideways through water.  I half-close my eyes and imagine that the moth is really a small fragment of that very whistle—a silver crumb of sound that lost its direction and decided to become luminous instead.

We begin things, all of us, under the illusion that we are steering.  In the first memory my mind can hold with any clarity, I am four years old, sitting in a sandbox that smells of damp cedar and cat urine.  I am flattening a moat around a castle with my palms, pressing the grains until they shine like mica.  An older boy approaches and informs me my castle is “wrong”; he demonstrates the proper slant of a parapet by kicking the lip of my moat until it caves.  My first architectural commission is demolished inside the length of a single cracked breath.  I remember thinking—although I would not have put it this way—how strangely careless the world seems toward anyone’s intention.  The train keeps rolling, the sand trickles through new fingers, the moth will always find its lamp.  No one asks whether the lamp consents to singe its wings.

That was the first ghost in the gallery of ancestors whose portraits I still carry: the ghost of the imperfect castle.  Henceforth, every essay, painting, garden, friendship, career choice, road trip, marriage, or apology would secretly begin from the premise: let me re-summon the sand and try again for a citadel no one will be able to kick down.  Of course the castle always topples; the great marvel is how many dazzling shapes it assumes before it does.  I have spent forty years designing better turrets, wider moats, stronger mortar, and softer greetings for strangers.  Forty years assembling wooden words and glass syllables, always in stealthy combat with the boot of the anonymous boy who levelled me at age four.  That bastard shaped more of my civic life than any mentor, any parent, any syllabus or sermon.  By the time I discovered he died in a motorcycle accident at nineteen, it was laughably too late to thank him or to be angry.  His single carefree kick in 1984 still sends shockwaves through the paragraph you are reading.

I try to keep the window cracked whenever I write, so the night air can push its own commas and asides into my sentences.  Some of them grow up to be line-breaks; others congeal into long silences that resemble the space when a record ends but the needle hasn’t yet been lifted.  Early on, I mistook those moments for failures of eloquence.  Later I learned that sometimes the say nothing is the final stanza the poem had been reaching for all along.  In the hush, I hear the particular timbre of my mother’s voice whenever she scolded me for reading at the dinner table.  “Put that book down and join the conversation!” she would insist, as if literature were some periphery I might wander back from.  She could not yet see that the table itself was already a verb in somebody else’s novel, that our forks rasped against porcelain like punctuation marks wrestling for dominance.  My father chewed in a minor key, sending small Morse code taps through the silverware; my sister’s milk glass conducted moonlight in slow translucence.  They thought they were maintaining the civility of a meal.  I was busy annotating gravity, plague years, the silence after Charlemagne sneezed.

My mother still lives two hours north, surrounded by the same pastel tissue-paper lampshades she bought at a yard sale near Quantico in 1978.  Sunday afternoons she sits in a rocker she will die in someday, gradually lowering the volume on the Game Show Network until it becomes a continuous breathy hush punctuated by distant correct answers.  I visit monthly, pretending to have urgent questions about her zwieback recipe, which I already transcribed years ago.  The real ritual is I sit at her feet and let her narrate the genealogy of every blemish in the carpet.  That iron burn happened when your father tried to press his uniform while drunk on rye; the rusted ellipsis near the sofa leg reveals where a mason jar of pennies toppled as she carried it to the bank during the recession; the beige bloom up near the threshold remembers a morning when her heart broke so cleanly she thought the soul was made of popcorn.  She speaks as though carpet stains were hieroglyphics verifying that we have indeed existed, that history is less a march of great men than a slow seep of tea, blood, and lemon extract through polypropylene loops.  Somewhere among the genealogy of spills is my own dispersed biography: a constellation of tears I cried at twelve when our dog was hit by a truck, chocolate milk I overturned in an ecstatic tantrum of adolescent longing, ashes of a letter my first love burned because she didn’t know of a safer incinerator.

Once, during graduate school, the carpet of a motel near Knoxville caught fire, and the news carried a photograph of blackened polyester rising in riptides of smoke.  I stared until I felt the fibers singeing a corresponding pattern across my own lungs.  What struck me was not the tragedy—nobody died—but the way the pattern of flame resembled a map of the constellations I used to look for through the dirty skylight over my childhood bed.  Orion’s scabbard reappeared in a clot of melted acrylic; the Pleiades did a ghost dance behind flickering chlorine fumes.  That night I wrote on a diner napkin the sentence that would become an epigraph for the dissertation that would never become a book: “Every ruin is a translation of sky into grammar.”  Years later I typed it over the doorway of a classroom, above the bored heads of students never born when the napkin absorbed a coffee ring like Saturn absorbing its own rings.

People sometimes ask whether I believe in free will.  The moth keeps batting the bulb as if it runs on a battery of furious consent; the train keeps rolling east; my mother keeps lowering the volume until even the applause sounds like long-distance grief.  In the middle of this firmament of momentum, a single word appears on my tongue: perhaps.  It tastes like abandoned penny, like rain arrived too late for the roses yet early enough for the peonies.  When I was seven I tried to catch “perhaps” beneath a Mason jar held up to afternoon light, convinced that if I could trap one meteorological uncertainty, all other varieties would honor the treaty and stay outside my chest.  The jar filled with sunlight, then dusk, then my own astonished breath condensed against the glass.  I had entrapped nothing but my further yearning to be elsewhere.  The adult version of that same experiment is marriage; the jar is reassuringly heavier, but the condensation is identical.

It strikes me that if I follow every tributary of memory far enough upstream, eventually I reach a space where language has not yet arrived.  That place crackles with possibility like light just before thunder.  I suppose this is why some thinkers locate God at the lip of the unsayable: the divine inhabits the silence no syllable can inhabit without rupturing.  Yet holiday dinners arrive with the unstoppable regularity of bad weather, and even monks must pass the gravy.  So we invent smaller gods: the deity of butter knives that always land blade-up in the drainer; the saint of mismatched socks who secretly knits them back together inside the drum of the dryer; the hearth-goddess of lint whose holy writ is written in fuzzy palimpsests dreaming of being dust again.  On the windowsill behind my laptop, the moth has decided the bulb is either too hot or too bright and benevolence.  It tumbles once, limp as an opened envelope, then revives and throws itself again at the white-hot stamp of illumination.

I want to talk about failure now because it is the plot no editor ever commissions yet it steals every narrative.  In the hierarchy of failures, mine are middle-weight: a besmirched credit score, three heartbreaks that escalated from fireworks to anti-fireworks, various rejections stapled together into a sad kite I occasionally pull out to see whether it can still manage a loop-de-loop.  None of these compares to the inverted cathedral of someone’s child dead before kindergarten.  Still, failure is not an absolute province; it is purified in the retelling.  At twenty-two I lost a scholarship because I forgot to lick the envelope that enclosed the renewal form.  That thin paper tongue never met the gummed edge.  For weeks I dreamed of envelopes receding at the speed of surf; I woke certain I could taste the glue of the cosmos unsealing.  I worked graveyard shift at a bakery to pay tuition.  One dawn, the industrial oven miscycled and two thousand bran muffins flattened into puck-sized fossils, each stamped with the twin f-holes of a violin.  My manager, an ex-philosophy major named Lucinda, regarded the steaming ruins and murmured, “Existence precedes pastry.”  We laughed so hard we forgot to weep, at least until the invoices came due.  Some nights I still hear the oven door clang shut behind possibility itself.

Success, meanwhile, is an exasperated librarian who keeps trying to re-shelve the same book under “Local History, 1990-1991” when what it really wants is the cart labelled “Apocrypha.”  The most successful thing I ever authored was a laminated map to the bicycle racks at a community college in Oregon.  A hundred thousand folds and refolds later, riders still use it.  The irony is I drew the map on the backs of lousy poems I’d culled from a folder marked “DO NOT SUBMIT.”  Those poems survive only as ghost tracery beneath blue arrows pointing left toward “Secure Lockers.”  Someone’s Schwinn leans against the rack today; the chain grease blurs a stanza that used to begin, “Let us inherit the windmill without inheriting the scream.”  I cannot decide whether this qualifies as resurrection or further burial.

Outside, the train has gone; its echo now navigates among distant foothills like a rumor searching for a body to inhabit.  I stand and stretch, joints crackling as if someone lit a tiny pine cone in each knee.  The moth has vanished—either fled or vaporized.  I prefer to picture it wafting back toward the whistle-that-became-it, carrying news of human incandescence to wide-eared cattle grazing by the tracks.  The room around me doesn’t dim, not exactly, but the quality of lamplight changes as the hour after midnight.  Molecules of air appear reassigned; silence itself is suddenly an octave higher.  A small galaxy of dust moats orbits above the desk.  I find myself apologizing to them aloud for forty years of inhaling their cousins.  

Childhood cancers bloom in the adjacent county like late hydrangeas.  Every news item brims with reasons to lurch toward despairing literalism.  “The world is ending tomorrow at 3:07 p.m.”  But tomorrow is a spoon bending at a séance—it snaps in the same motion it elongates.  While the debate stage seethes and hectopascal-countermemes congeal, sparrows drink from puddles shaped like auditory canals, proving that smallness survives by refusing invitation to the apocalypse.  My partner’s breathing in the next room travels down the hallway and curls around my ankle like a cat that hasn’t decided whether to bite tenderly or simply settle.  I reach down with one hand to acknowledge its presence.

There are 47 keys on this keyboard; one sticks slightly (the letter H).  Each time I type it, I am met with a micro-resistance that feels almost human, as if the key is reluctant to abandon its non-historical hush and join this babble after midnight.  I whisper thank you every time it finally yields.  This is how I honor the quotidian: by publicly noticing its bruises.  Likewise, the neighbor’s porch light burns a sulfurous orange every single night regardless of whether anyone is watching.  Ten years ago that same lamp flickered unpredictably, reminding all of insomnia that unpredictability is the last remaining spice.  The Homeowner’s Association sent letters.  Then one afternoon an electrician showed up, and since then the light has been as loyal as dogma.  Sometimes I walk the dog past at 3 a.m. just to salute its bizarre fidelity to order.  It never flickers back; it simply glows, a monastic vow in sodium form.  I find that disproportionately consoling.

Have you noticed how, in the tidal zone between sleeping and waking, every small anxiety puffs itself into a leviathan wearing your high-school geometry teacher’s eyeglasses?  I have lost honest sleep to arranging hypothetical seating charts around an imaginary banquet whose theme is “Every Regret You Ever Owned, Each Seated Across From Its Corresponding Forgiveness.”  The regrets arrive first, lugging gravy boats filled with gall.  The forgivers come later, barefoot, slightly drunk on the odd mercy of being invited at all.  Last night the ghost of my ninth-grade plagiarized book report sat between an apology I never sent and my father’s first cigarette after his heart attack.  No utensils appeared; we had to drink light from one another’s hands.  Somewhere in the dialogue that ensued, the geometry teacher—who in real life died at forty-seven—removed her glasses and said, “The shortest distance between any two points is memory.”  Then the dream collapsed like a folding chair.  I woke with her glasses folded beside my pillow as cold as absentia.  I have yet to decide whether to place them in a drawer or hang them from the rearview mirror of every future regret.

There are entire weeks when I forget to look up at the sky, only to realize one Thursday that it has been staging operas above me in a language I used to understand perfectly at age seven.  The vocabulary returns as soon as I tilt my head: cirrus is “grandmother’s shawl,” cumulonimbus is “father’s stubborn sermon,”  Every refresher course costs me an instant of vertigo because to crane my neck is to admit how large the surrounding abyss truly is.  Better the consoling square of the window frame, better the moth-shaped hole where danger has already scorched its witness onto the bulb.

The coffee I brewed at 10 p.m. to meet this deadline has turned cold, finding in its own miniature caldera the thick skin of ideas gone lukewarm.  Outside the sine curve of the interstate sighs politely, conducting strangers to cities where other lamps burn over other desks over other writers who have likewise underestimated how quickly caffeine reverts to stasis.  In each of those rooms the same imperceptible arithmetic is unfolding: how much carbon based sorrow can one laptop depreciate before spark and silence reconcile?  The question refuses to sit politely for a portrait; instead it scuttles like a nursery-rhyme spider across the page even as I attempt its autopsy with acetylene verbs.

I have read that astronauts returning from orbit must relearn the posture of standing because their vestibular systems misplace verticality.  I wonder what freedom from gravity feels like when the true anchor is not orientation but expectation.  What if weightlessness is simply arriving at the moment when you no longer ask the floor why it keeps betraying you?  I experiment with absolving the floor while stubbing my toe on it tomorrow—the non-dual version of insult turned love token.  In anticipation, the carpet fibers bristle as if someone just whispered a secret about laundry metaphysics into the nap.

There exists a species of orchid whose petals resemble the image of a female wasp.  Male wasps, deceived, attempt copulation—thereby pollinating the flower.  Biologists call the phenomenon “reproductive deceit.”  I think of it whenever a memoirist admits, “Here is the truth as I remember it,” because what is any memoir but an exquisitely evolved petal threatening to address some lonely wasp of the present?  The reader flies in, desperate to mate with the past; the story consents to be pollenized and thus reborn as art.  Swindle blesses continuation.  Yet even the pollinator retains blurry proof that salvation sometimes wears the mask of fraud.

Two streets over, the widower is watering his garden at 1:07 a.m.  I know this because sometimes our insomnia overlaps like Venn diagrams of ache.  His hose is rigged with a sprinkler shaped like a sunflower that pirouettes, flinging diamonds onto zinnias no one will see until daylight receipts them into fluorescence.  We are neighbors in the sympathetic nervous system: we both fear and crave the moment when everything shuts down long enough for us to notice what survived.   Last summer he called me over to identify a bug drowning in a bucket.  It was a hummingbird moth—same species as my late-night visitor—deceived by mirror-brightness into thinking water was air.  I cupped it gently; its wings dried into infrared on my palm.  When I released it, the widower nodded as though I had just signed a warrant for his continued allegiance to sunrise.  He said nothing, yet something finished its sentence in the negative space between us.  I walked home under the overhead sycamore that keeps trying to reenact the birth of stars, each pinprick leaf glowing with borrowed nocturne.

The first time I misunderstood death was-to-strut ratio was when my grandfather, a retired mail carrier, demonstrated how to sharpen a pencil with a pocketknife by feeling alone.  His hands shook ever so lightly, but the blade shaved graphite into moons of violet revelation.  “The secret,” he said, “is not to stop when the sharpness looks adequate.  Sharpen until the point fears its own reflection, then let it forgive itself on paper.”  Decades later I realized he was paraphrasing Marcus Aurelius without knowing a single Stoic syllable.  After the funeral, his pencils remained in a tin box beneath his bed, each nib blunted by the intervening requiem.  I took one, pressed it to a notebook, and the lead snapped in abbreviated grief.  It was the least ceremonial burial my wrists have ever staged.

I have sometimes wondered why humans invented the exclamation mark.  Perhaps punctuation itself is the history of our fear that the voice will be misheard across the centuries.  In hieroglyphs, upside-down avocados meant jubilation; in conversation, italics appear only as elongated vowels soaked in embarrassment.  The exclamation mark, then, is a knitting needle hooked through the eyelid of despair to keep it from shut-ting.  It says: still here.  I never deploy it without tasting iron.  If you find one at the end of this sentence, it is merely the sound of the train arriving somewhere I cannot see.

At seventeen I signed a pledge to become a mechanical engineer because octagons and levers matched the internal shape my anxiety had assumed.  Instead, I found myself electrocuting a capacitive discharge welder in lab class while Daft Punk thumped from the professor’s gummy earbuds.  The voltage flung my eyelashes outward like exclamation marks, singeing them into commas.  The professor’s response was to lower the volume and say, “Don’t anthropomorphize machines—they hate that.”  We laughed; later, at 3 a.m. in the dormitory dormancy, I wrote a poem in which every sprocket memorized the exact blue of a vanished lake.  That week I switched majors from engineering to Lit, trading certainty for paper cuts that never quite fatal, more comparable to love bites from imaginary nymphs.

A friend of mine from those years now designs algorithms that predict opioid overdoses in real time, scrolling through anonymized geolocation dots that flare scarlet at 2 a.m. in a McDonald’s bathroom.  He calls the program “Eleos,” after the Greek goddess of mercy.  I met him for coffee six months ago; he showed me a heat map of the city we both grew up in, now pulsing red along the river where we used to skip stones at fifteen.  He muttered, “See those clumps?  Half are still alive as of 6 a.m., half are not.”  The barista misspelled Eleos on the receipt as “Eloise,” and for a second I pictured the goddess registering as a guest at a Marriott, requesting extra towels for the inconsolable.

Tonight I recall that heat map while heating soup for tomorrow’s thermos.  The stove flame is exactly the blue of the widower’s pre-dawn watering, the same shade that hovered between my grand-father’s thumb and forefinger while the pencil surrendered its wood.  Every transformation begins with the permission to be changed.  The lentil soup gurgles like a stutterer who has finally been listened to.  Salt meets protein meets memory of my Lithuanian great-aunt whose kitchen smelled of caraway and arsenic dread.  hunger was the first continent we colonized merely by heating intention.  Tonight I salt the lentils for tomorrow’s lunch of the students who will arrive half-asleep to discuss impermanence inside a windowless room.  Perhaps the soup will be smoother; perhaps the students will be hungrier; perhaps the temperature between spoon lip and tongue will confuse the boundary between lecture and lullaby.

While the pot simmers I dial my mother.  She recites senate hearings at me like bedtime stories, every “treasonous” and “undeniable” replacing “once upon a time.”  Her voice lilts up in questions no lawyer ever asked when we believed in due process.  She forgets, every fifth minute, that I am on the phone and starts addressing the television.  I don’t take offense; it is another rehearsal for when her memory will fully decentralize.  The infomercial hawking clip-on magnifying lenses replies to her with the identical cadence every half hour, and eventually I realize the man in the ad has become her interlocutor, her last steady pen pal.  We hang up agreeing that the magnifying lenses indeed “clip on in seconds without obstructing your peripheral vision.”  My mother’s yes dissolves into static resembling applause by a thinning congregation.  The soup is done.  I ladle it into mason jars while the neighbor’s sprinkler achieves its nightly transcendence of zinnia choreography.  

Midnight snacks for the philosophy of impermanence: cold jar of peas balanced on a Saul Bellow novel; spoonful of honey to sweeten the sleeve of my robe; a ghost crouton floating like a tiny square dimension ship.  While chewing I open the browser to search “how to conduct a moth funeral” and find instead instructions on migrating your consciousness into a vivarium peppered with nursery-ready stars.  I close the laptop.  Apparently heaven is now available in beta release; I prefer the moth’s version—one gust toward incineration, then luminous dispersal.

At 2:13 a.m. a crack like breaking celery issues from behind the refrigerator, and I realize the ice maker has achieved its nightly quota.  Refrigeration is the loveliest form of suspended matrimony: water marries cold, they adopt solid shape, they file adoption papers for the dream of a dry martini.  The cube tumbles in with all the arrogance of a fact.  I picture that cylinder striking the bin during dinosaur times when glaciers themselves were the ice cubes of planetary thirst.  Time is a warehouse of cooling systems none of us consented to lease.  I swipe a cube, wrap it in a napkin, and place it atop the blister on my palm where today’s ladle slipped.  The blister hisses slightly, offering benediction.

There’s a moment right after the wound in your hand meets the cold when the body forgets it owns a biography longer than this single minute.  I close my eyes to cherish the confiscation.  Briefly I am neither son nor mourner nor citizen trying to negotiate the terms of continued globe tenancy.  I am merely temperature negotiating barter with skin.  When the cube melts, memory begins its tedious correspondences again—this time delivering a snapshot of a rain gutter in monsoon season overflowing with dead bees.  Childhood allergy doctor’s visit enters holding hands with the rumor of an island coast sinking like a failed soufflé, everyone evacuated except a poet arguing with the tide about where one line should break.

The hands on the wall clock hesitate at 2:27, then tick twice in the same second, as though apologizing to the moon for miscounting a heartbeat.  Somewhere someone is high above Kansas altitude, watching auroral curtains shift like spectral orthodonture correcting misaligned star-spines.  Somewhere else a midwife mutters an ancient blessing over an infant whose first gulp of atmosphere includes molecules once exhaled by Galileo.  The telephone wire outside my window faintly vibrates even though no one’s called the landline in years.  Everything is singing through tension right now: aluminum alloy, the spider drunk on infrared drafts, the overcaffeinated cardiac thumps beneath my sternum—each vibrating slightly off key so that together they produce an accidental chord called survival.

My partner appears in the doorway wearing the look that says: yes, the soup was good but not good enough to forgive you for tripping over the dog’s water bowl en route to bed.  Tomorrow the dog will drink from floorboards; tonight he nestles under the table awaiting forgiveness that will come in the form of tusks of cold toast.  Love fractures, mends, elevates mundane shame to brotherhood of basement jokes.  She kisses my temple—salt, regret, lentils, mercy in each brush of lip.  We do not discuss the heat map, or the kitchen clock’s double tick, or the moth possibly exploring nirvana inside the neighbor’s porch light.  We simply walk the hallway in semi-dark, guided more by contour than concept, two silhouettes practicing the difficult symmetry of returning.

I’ve reached the age where lists begin to seduce me with the illusion that compendiums equal continuance.  A partial inventory of what survives in pockets of jackets seldom worn: ticket stub from a Beck concert 1997; a blue guitar pick belonging to nobody I know; a single puzzle piece shaped like Kentucky; a metro card for a city I lost my virginity in; dryer lint laminated between scotch tape; and a fortune I keep forgetting to mail to a friend who believes wholeheartedly in fortunes.  The lint was once fibers scraped off every garment that ever touched my skin, now pressed into a coin made of ourselves.  One day I’ll hole-punch it, thread ribbon through, wear it as pendant until the dryer reclaims itself piece by mitotic off-white piece.  Jewelry for bureaucrats of chaos; currency for the nation of almost.

The soup cools into its next geological stratum; the day that begins in four hours will lay sediment on top.  I will lecture about metaphor, and halfway into the hour a student will raise her hand to say, “My grandmother used to thaw river ice with her breath alone during famine.”  My curated syllabus will feel suddenly fraudulent.  I will lean against the whiteboard like a man remembering how plywood eventually delaminates.  A window will rattle exactly the way my mother once rattled soup bones in tupperware to summon broth.  The student will lower her gaze, embarrassed by the divine alignment.  I will say nothing eloquent.  Instead I will open the thermos, pour lentils into a paper cup, and say, “Here, eat,” thereby handing the period over to chickpeas.

At 3:04 a.m. the thermostat clicks, the radiator sighs, the world rehearses small hushes designed for no human ear.  Now is when I should extrude a final moral or at least hint at the hotel where plotlines convene for breakfast after climax.  But plotting is the only profession more futile than meteorology in springtime.  Instead let me leave you with a new unopened box of tea, the kind whose perforated bag releases jasmine steam like tiny footsteps escaping verse.  Somewhere offstage a kettle finally concludes its aria.  Someone lifts it mid-phrase, the living-room equivalent of a conductor halting Wagner before Valhalla falls.  The ensuing hush is perfect, angular, made of future directions stitched out of silence.  

If you choose to visit tomorrow, bring the memory of your own almost-castle.  We will pour lentils into mismatched bowls, forgive ourselves for the inevitable collapse.  The train may or may not pass, the moth will have already negotiated whatever treaty light requires, and maybe—just maybe—the floor will forgive our hooves long enough for us to reimagine gravity as mutual hospitality.

When I was seven I tried to catch “perhaps” beneath a Mason jar held up to afternoon light, convinced that if I could trap one meteorological uncertainty, all other varieties would honor the treaty and stay outside my chest.  The jar filled with sunlight, then dusk, then my own astonished breath condensed against the glass.  I had entrapped nothing but my further yearning to be elsewhere.  The adult version of that same experiment is marriage; the jar is reassuringly heavier, but the condensation is identical.

It strikes me that if I follow every tributary of memory far enough upstream, eventually I reach a space where language has not yet arrived.  That place crackles with possibility like light just before thunder.  I suppose this is why some thinkers locate God at the lip of the unsayable: the divine inhabits the silence no syllable can inhabit without rupturing.  Yet holiday dinners arrive with the unstoppable regularity of bad weather, and even monks must pass the gravy.  So we invent smaller gods: the deity of butter knives that always land blade-up in the drainer; the saint of mismatched socks who secretly knits them back together inside the drum of the dryer; the hearth-goddess of lint whose holy writ is written in fuzzy palimpsests dreaming of being dust again.  On the windowsill behind my laptop, the moth has decided the bulb is either too hot or too bright and benevolence.  It tumbles once, limp as an opened envelope, then revives and throws itself again at the white-hot stamp of illumination.

I want to talk about failure now because it is the plot no editor ever commissions yet it steals every narrative.  In the hierarchy of failures, mine are middle-weight: a besmirched credit score, three heartbreaks that escalated from fireworks to anti-fireworks, various rejections stapled together into a sad kite I occasionally pull out to see whether it can still manage a loop-de-loop.  None of these compares to the inverted cathedral of someone’s child dead before kindergarten.  Still, failure is not an absolute province; it is purified in the retelling.  At twenty-two I lost a scholarship because I forgot to lick the envelope that enclosed the renewal form.  That thin paper tongue never met the gummed edge.  For weeks I dreamed of envelopes receding at the speed of surf; I woke certain I could taste the glue of the cosmos unsealing.  I worked graveyard shift at a bakery to pay tuition.  One dawn, the industrial oven miscycled and two thousand bran muffins flattened into puck-sized fossils, each stamped with the twin f-holes of a violin.  My manager, an ex-philosophy major named Lucinda, regarded the steaming ruins and murmured, “Existence precedes pastry.”  We laughed so hard we forgot to weep, at least until the invoices came due.  Some nights I still hear the oven door clang shut behind possibility itself.

Success, meanwhile, is an exasperated librarian who keeps trying to re-shelve the same book under “Local History, 1990-1991” when what it really wants is the cart labelled “Apocrypha.”  The most successful thing I ever authored was a laminated map to the bicycle racks at a community college in Oregon.  A hundred thousand folds and refolds later, riders still use it.  The irony is I drew the map on the backs of lousy poems I’d culled from a folder marked “DO NOT SUBMIT.”  Those poems survive only as ghost tracery beneath blue arrows pointing left toward “Secure Lockers.”  Someone’s Schwinn leans against the rack today; the chain grease blurs a stanza that used to begin, “Let us inherit the windmill without inheriting the scream.”  I cannot decide whether this qualifies as resurrection or further burial.

Outside, the train has gone; its echo now navigates among distant foothills like a rumor searching for a body to inhabit.  I stand and stretch, joints crackling as if someone lit a tiny pine cone in each knee.  The moth has vanished—either fled or vaporized.  I prefer to picture it wafting back toward the whistle-that-became-it, carrying news of human incandescence to wide-eared cattle grazing by the tracks.  The room around me doesn’t dim, not exactly, but the quality of lamplight changes as the hour after midnight.  Molecules of air appear reassigned; silence itself is suddenly an octave higher.  A small galaxy of dust moats orbits above the desk.  I find myself apologizing to them aloud for forty years of inhaling their cousins.  

Childhood cancers bloom in the adjacent county like late hydrangeas.  Every news item brims with reasons to lurch toward despairing literalism.  “The world is ending tomorrow at 3:07 p.m.”  But tomorrow is a spoon bending at a séance—it snaps in the same motion it elongates.  While the debate stage seethes and hectopascal-countermemes congeal, sparrows drink from puddles shaped like auditory canals, proving that smallness survives by refusing invitation to the apocalypse.  My partner’s breathing in the next room travels down the hallway and curls around my ankle like a cat that hasn’t decided whether to bite tenderly or simply settle.  I reach down with one hand to acknowledge its presence.

There are 47 keys on this keyboard; one sticks slightly (the letter H).  Each time I type it, I am met with a micro-resistance that feels almost human, as if the key is reluctant to abandon its non-historical hush and join this babble after midnight.  I whisper thank you every time it finally yields.  This is how I honor the quotidian: by publicly noticing its bruises.  Likewise, the neighbor’s porch light burns a sulfurous orange every single night regardless of whether anyone is watching.  Ten years ago that same lamp flickered unpredictably, reminding all of insomnia that unpredictability is the last remaining spice.  The Homeowner’s Association sent letters.  Then one afternoon an electrician showed up, and since then the light has been as loyal as dogma.  Sometimes I walk the dog past at 3 a.m. just to salute its bizarre fidelity to order.  It never flickers back; it simply glows, a monastic vow in sodium form.  I find that disproportionately consoling.

Have you noticed how, in the tidal zone between sleeping and waking, every small anxiety puffs itself into a leviathan wearing your high-school geometry teacher’s eyeglasses?  I have lost honest sleep to arranging hypothetical seating charts around an imaginary banquet whose theme is “Every Regret You Ever Owned, Each Seated Across From Its Corresponding Forgiveness.”  The regrets arrive first, lugging gravy boats filled with gall.  The forgivers come later, barefoot, slightly drunk on the odd mercy of being invited at all.  Last night the ghost of my ninth-grade plagiarized book report sat between an apology I never sent and my father’s first cigarette after his heart attack.  No utensils appeared; we had to drink light from one another’s hands.  Somewhere in the dialogue that ensued, the geometry teacher—who in real life died at forty-seven—removed her glasses and said, “The shortest distance between any two points is memory.”  Then the dream collapsed like a folding chair.  I woke with her glasses folded beside my pillow as cold as absentia.  I have yet to decide whether to place them in a drawer or hang them from the rearview mirror of every future regret.

There are entire weeks when I forget to look up at the sky, only to realize one Thursday that it has been staging operas above me in a language I used to understand perfectly at age seven.  The vocabulary returns as soon as I tilt my head: cirrus is “grandmother’s shawl,” cumulonimbus is “father’s stubborn sermon,”  Every refresher course costs me an instant of vertigo because to crane my neck is to admit how large the surrounding abyss truly is.  Better the consoling square of the window frame, better the moth-shaped hole where danger has already scorched its witness onto the bulb.

The coffee I brewed at 10 p.m. to meet this deadline has turned cold, finding in its own miniature caldera the thick skin of ideas gone lukewarm.  Outside the sine curve of the interstate sighs politely, conducting strangers to cities where other lamps burn over other desks over other writers who have likewise underestimated how quickly caffeine reverts to stasis.  In each of those rooms the same imperceptible arithmetic is unfolding: how much carbon based sorrow can one laptop depreciate before spark and silence reconcile?  The question refuses to sit politely for a portrait; instead it scuttles like a nursery-rhyme spider across the page even as I attempt its autopsy with acetylene verbs.

I have read that astronauts returning from orbit must relearn the posture of standing because their vestibular systems misplace verticality.  I wonder what freedom from gravity feels like when the true anchor is not orientation but expectation.  What if weightlessness is simply arriving at the moment when you no longer ask the floor why it keeps betraying you?  I experiment with absolving the floor while stubbing my toe on it tomorrow—the non-dual version of insult turned love token.  In anticipation, the carpet fibers bristle as if someone just whispered a secret about laundry metaphysics into the nap.

There exists a species of orchid whose petals resemble the image of a female wasp.  Male wasps, deceived, attempt copulation—thereby pollinating the flower.  Biologists call the phenomenon “reproductive deceit.”  I think of it whenever a memoirist admits, “Here is the truth as I remember it,” because what is any memoir but an exquisitely evolved petal threatening to address some lonely wasp of the present?  The reader flies in, desperate to mate with the past; the story consents to be pollenized and thus reborn as art.  Swindle blesses continuation.  Yet even the pollinator retains blurry proof that salvation sometimes wears the mask of fraud.

Two streets over, the widower is watering his garden at 1:07 a.m.  I know this because sometimes our insomnia overlaps like Venn diagrams of ache.  His hose is rigged with a sprinkler shaped like a sunflower that pirouettes, flinging diamonds onto zinnias no one will see until daylight receipts them into fluorescence.  We are neighbors in the sympathetic nervous system: we both fear and crave the moment when everything shuts down long enough for us to notice what survived.   Last summer he called me over to identify a bug drowning in a bucket.  It was a hummingbird moth—same species as my late-night visitor—deceived by mirror-brightness into thinking water was air.  I cupped it gently; its wings dried into infrared on my palm.  When I released it, the widower nodded as though I had just signed a warrant for his continued allegiance to sunrise.  He said nothing, yet something finished its sentence in the negative space between us.  I walked home under the overhead sycamore that keeps trying to reenact the birth of stars, each pinprick leaf glowing with borrowed nocturne.

The first time I misunderstood death was-to-strut ratio was when my grandfather, a retired mail carrier, demonstrated how to sharpen a pencil with a pocketknife by feeling alone.  His hands shook ever so lightly, but the blade shaved graphite into moons of violet revelation.  “The secret,” he said, “is not to stop when the sharpness looks adequate.  Sharpen until the point fears its own reflection, then let it forgive itself on paper.”  Decades later I realized he was paraphrasing Marcus Aurelius without knowing a single Stoic syllable.  After the funeral, his pencils remained in a tin box beneath his bed, each nib blunted by the intervening requiem.  I took one, pressed it to a notebook, and the lead snapped in abbreviated grief.  It was the least ceremonial burial my wrists have ever staged.

I have sometimes wondered why humans invented the exclamation mark.  Perhaps punctuation itself is the history of our fear that the voice will be misheard across the centuries.  In hieroglyphs, upside-down avocados meant jubilation; in conversation, italics appear only as elongated vowels soaked in embarrassment.  The exclamation mark, then, is a knitting needle hooked through the eyelid of despair to keep it from shut-ting.  It says: still here.  I never deploy it without tasting iron.  If you find one at the end of this sentence, it is merely the sound of the train arriving somewhere I cannot see.

At seventeen I signed a pledge to become a mechanical engineer because octagons and levers matched the internal shape my anxiety had assumed.  Instead, I found myself electrocuting a capacitive discharge welder in lab class while Daft Punk thumped from the professor’s gummy earbuds.  The voltage flung my eyelashes outward like exclamation marks, singeing them into commas.  The professor’s response was to lower the volume and say, “Don’t anthropomorphize machines—they hate that.”  We laughed; later, at 3 a.m. in the dormitory dormancy, I wrote a poem in which every sprocket memorized the exact blue of a vanished lake.  That week I switched majors from engineering to Lit, trading certainty for paper cuts that never quite fatal, more comparable to love bites from imaginary nymphs.

A friend of mine from those years now designs algorithms that predict opioid overdoses in real time, scrolling through anonymized geolocation dots that flare scarlet at 2 a.m. in a McDonald’s bathroom.  He calls the program “Eleos,” after the Greek goddess of mercy.  I met him for coffee six months ago; he showed me a heat map of the city we both grew up in, now pulsing red along the river where we used to skip stones at fifteen.  He muttered, “See those clumps?  Half are still alive as of 6 a.m., half are not.”  The barista misspelled Eleos on the receipt as “Eloise,” and for a second I pictured the goddess registering as a guest at a Marriott, requesting extra towels for the inconsolable.

Tonight I recall that heat map while heating soup for tomorrow’s thermos.  The stove flame is exactly the blue of the widower’s pre-dawn watering, the same shade that hovered between my grand-father’s thumb and forefinger while the pencil surrendered its wood.  Every transformation begins with the permission to be changed.  The lentil soup gurgles like a stutterer who has finally been listened to.  Salt meets protein meets memory of my Lithuanian great-aunt whose kitchen smelled of caraway and arsenic dread.  hunger was the first continent we colonized merely by heating intention.  Tonight I salt the lentils for tomorrow’s lunch of the students who will arrive half-asleep to discuss impermanence inside a windowless room.  Perhaps the soup will be smoother; perhaps the students will be hungrier; perhaps the temperature between spoon lip and tongue will confuse the boundary between lecture and lullaby.

While the pot simmers I dial my mother.  She recites senate hearings at me like bedtime stories, every “treasonous” and “undeniable” replacing “once upon a time.”  Her voice lilts up in questions no lawyer ever asked when we believed in due process.  She forgets, every fifth minute, that I am on the phone and starts addressing the television.  I don’t take offense; it is another rehearsal for when her memory will fully decentralize.  The infomercial hawking clip-on magnifying lenses replies to her with the identical cadence every half hour, and eventually I realize the man in the ad has become her interlocutor, her last steady pen pal.  We hang up agreeing that the magnifying lenses indeed “clip on in seconds without obstructing your peripheral vision.”  My mother’s yes dissolves into static resembling applause by a thinning congregation.  The soup is done.  I ladle it into mason jars while the neighbor’s sprinkler achieves its nightly transcendence of zinnia choreography.  

Midnight snacks for the philosophy of impermanence: cold jar of peas balanced on a Saul Bellow novel; spoonful of honey to sweeten the sleeve of my robe; a ghost crouton floating like a tiny square dimension ship.  While chewing I open the browser to search “how to conduct a moth funeral” and find instead instructions on migrating your consciousness into a vivarium peppered with nursery-ready stars.  I close the laptop.  Apparently heaven is now available in beta release; I prefer the moth’s version—one gust toward incineration, then luminous dispersal.

At 2:13 a.m. a crack like breaking celery issues from behind the refrigerator, and I realize the ice maker has achieved its nightly quota.  Refrigeration is the loveliest form of suspended matrimony: water marries cold, they adopt solid shape, they file adoption papers for the dream of a dry martini.  The cube tumbles in with all the arrogance of a fact.  I picture that cylinder striking the bin during dinosaur times when glaciers themselves were the ice cubes of planetary thirst.  Time is a warehouse of cooling systems none of us consented to lease.  I swipe a cube, wrap it in a napkin, and place it atop the blister on my palm where today’s ladle slipped.  The blister hisses slightly, offering benediction.

There’s a moment right after the wound in your hand meets the cold when the body forgets it owns a biography longer than this single minute.  I close my eyes to cherish the confiscation.  Briefly I am neither son nor mourner nor citizen trying to negotiate the terms of continued globe tenancy.  I am merely temperature negotiating barter with skin.  When the cube melts, memory begins its tedious correspondences again—this time delivering a snapshot of a rain gutter in monsoon season overflowing with dead bees.  Childhood allergy doctor’s visit enters holding hands with the rumor of an island coast sinking like a failed soufflé, everyone evacuated except a poet arguing with the tide about where one line should break.

The hands on the wall clock hesitate at 2:27, then tick twice in the same second, as though apologizing to the moon for miscounting a heartbeat.  Somewhere someone is high above Kansas altitude, watching auroral curtains shift like spectral orthodonture correcting misaligned star-spines.  Somewhere else a midwife mutters an ancient blessing over an infant whose first gulp of atmosphere includes molecules once exhaled by Galileo.  The telephone wire outside my window faintly vibrates even though no one’s called the landline in years.  Everything is singing through tension right now: aluminum alloy, the spider drunk on infrared drafts, the overcaffeinated cardiac thumps beneath my sternum—each vibrating slightly off key so that together they produce an accidental chord called survival.

My partner appears in the doorway wearing the look that says: yes, the soup was good but not good enough to forgive you for tripping over the dog’s water bowl en route to bed.  Tomorrow the dog will drink from floorboards; tonight he nestles under the table awaiting forgiveness that will come in the form of tusks of cold toast.  Love fractures, mends, elevates mundane shame to brotherhood of basement jokes.  She kisses my temple—salt, regret, lentils, mercy in each brush of lip.  We do not discuss the heat map, or the kitchen clock’s double tick, or the moth possibly exploring nirvana inside the neighbor’s porch light.  We simply walk the hallway in semi-dark, guided more by contour than concept, two silhouettes practicing the difficult symmetry of returning.

I’ve reached the age where lists begin to seduce me with the illusion that compendiums equal continuance.  A partial inventory of what survives in pockets of jackets seldom worn: ticket stub from a Beck concert 1997; a blue guitar pick belonging to nobody I know; a single puzzle piece shaped like Kentucky; a metro card for a city I lost my virginity in; dryer lint laminated between scotch tape; and a fortune I keep forgetting to mail to a friend who believes wholeheartedly in fortunes.  The lint was once fibers scraped off every garment that ever touched my skin, now pressed into a coin made of ourselves.  One day I’ll hole-punch it, thread ribbon through, wear it as pendant until the dryer reclaims itself piece by mitotic off-white piece.  Jewelry for bureaucrats of chaos; currency for the nation of almost.

The soup cools into its next geological stratum; the day that begins in four hours will lay sediment on top.  I will lecture about metaphor, and halfway into the hour a student will raise her hand to say, “My grandmother used to thaw river ice with her breath alone during famine.”  My curated syllabus will feel suddenly fraudulent.  I will lean against the whiteboard like a man remembering how plywood eventually delaminates.  A window will rattle exactly the way my mother once rattled soup bones in tupperware to summon broth.  The student will lower her gaze, embarrassed by the divine alignment.  I will say nothing eloquent.  Instead I will open the thermos, pour lentils into a paper cup, and say, “Here, eat,” thereby handing the period over to chickpeas.

At 3:04 a.m. the thermostat clicks, the radiator sighs, the world rehearses small hushes designed for no human ear.  Now is when I should extrude a final moral or at least hint at the hotel where plotlines convene for breakfast after climax.  But plotting is the only profession more futile than meteorology in springtime.  Instead let me leave you with a new unopened box of tea, the kind whose perforated bag releases jasmine steam like tiny footsteps escaping verse.  Somewhere offstage a kettle finally concludes its aria.  Someone lifts it mid-phrase, the living-room equivalent of a conductor halting Wagner before Valhalla falls.  The ensuing hush is perfect, angular, made of future directions stitched out of silence.  

If you choose to visit tomorrow, bring the memory of your own almost-castle.  We will pour lentils into mismatched bowls, forgive ourselves for the inevitable collapse.  The train may or may not pass, the moth will have already negotiated whatever treaty light requires, and maybe—just maybe—the floor will forgive our hooves long enough for us to reimagine gravity as mutual hospitality.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
